coredata.dc:description: Power conversion efficiency (PCE) of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has reached 26.1%, but PSC devices are plagued by poor stability when exposed to light (especially ultraviolet (UV) radiation), heat, and moisture. UV stability remains a significant challenge to overcome. Luminescent down-shifting (LDS) filters have shown significant enhancement in photostability and efficiency for PSCs. However, most explored LDS materials are costly, non-biodegradable, and the resulting photostability is limited to ∼100 h. In this report, as-obtained waste filtrate from the polyaniline (PANI) synthesis is used to synthesize fluorescent PANI carbon quantum dots (PANI-CQDs) using a facile hydrothermal method. Here we report, for the first time, the use of waste-derived PANI-CQDs to fabricate UV filters that are low-cost, bio-degradable, and room-temperature processible and, importantly, impart high UV and photostability to the PSCs. PSCs with these filters retained 90% and 100% of their initial performance when exposed to UV light and AM 1.5 solar radiation, respectively, for more than 900 h, while PSCs without filters degraded to 14 and 70% of their initial performance under the same conditions. Hence, we clearly show that using a waste-derived LDS filter improves the UV stability of PSCs by six times and photostability beyond 1,000 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM image of hybrid PANI-CQDs with a diameter ∼100 nm; (b) HRSTEM image of a single hybrid PANI-CQD, several nanocrystalline CQDs appear to agglomerate together to form hybrid PANI-CQDs; (c) image is obtained zooming inside the red box marked in (b) image; (d) lattice spacings associated with graphitic planes are identified, selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern with bright spots signifying the highly crystalline nature of PANI-CQDs; (e) AFM image of fresh PUF(CQDs-13.95 wt%); and (f) AFM image of fresh PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%). FESEM, field emission scanning electron microscopy; PANI-CODs, polyaniline-carbon quantum dots; HRSTEM, High-resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy; AFM, atomic force microscopy; PUF, PANI UV filter.

(a) FTIR spectra of PANI-CQDs obtained after hydrothermal synthesis of filtered or washed PANI material; (b) FTIR spectra of a thin film of PANI UV filter (composite of CQDs and polyvinyl alcohol); (c) comparison of absorbance of different types of PANI UV filters with a different weight percentage of PANI-CQDs; and (d) comparison of PL spectra of the filter with different excitation wavelengths in UV region from 295 nm to 390 nm. FTIR, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy; PANI-CODs, polyaniline-carbon quantum dots.

Shows the FESEM images of fresh and degraded triple-cation perovskite films in UV or one sun spectra in N2 environment (O2 < 0.5 ppm (avg), H2O = 0.1 ppm) without and with filter: (a) fresh film; (b) degraded triple-cation perovskite film w/o filter in UV light after 800 h; (c) degraded triple cation perovskite film with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter in UV light after 800 h; (d) degraded triple cation perovskite film w/o filter in AM 1.5 radiation after 1,100 h; and (e) degraded triple cation perovskite film with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter in AM 1.5 radiation after 1,100 h. FESEM, field emission scanning electron microscopy; PUF, PANI UV filter; CQDs, carbon quantum dots.

(a) XRD graphs of fresh and degraded triple cation perovskite films with and without PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter in UV light after 260 h; (b) The graph of (Fig. 4(a)) is shown in a range of 10° to 15° to compare their picks of α, δ, and PbI2 phase; and (c) The crystallinity of fresh and degraded triple cation perovskite films with and without PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter in UV light after 260 h are compared in this graph by analyzing their XRD data. XRD, X-ray diffraction; PUF, PANI UV filter; CQDs, carbon quantum dots.

(a) Schematic illustrates the PUF of PANI-CQDs-PVA composite fabricated on a glass substrate and PSCs setup in which the UV filter blocks the UV light and only allows the visible light to reach the PSCs. The cell has n-i-p architecture with triple-cation perovskite as the absorber layer; (b) The FESEM image of the cross-sectional area of PANI-CQDs-PVA composite fabricated on glass substrate and PSCs setup; (c) The performance losses of triple cation PSCs after using PUFs with different PANI-CQDs concentrations. Comparison of normalized PCE(%) of triple cation PSCs without and with a different type of PUFs with different weight percentages (0%, 3.98%, 5.68%, 7.76%, and 11.22%) of PANI-CQDs; (d) Comparison of J-V graphs of triple cation PSCs after reverse and forward scanning without and with a filter PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%); and (e) comparison of EQE graphs of triple cation PSCs without and with a filter PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) and their integrated short circuit current density from EQE graphs are also compared and shown in this graph. PUF, PANI UV filter; PANI-CODs, polyaniline-carbon quantum dots; PVA, polyvinyl alcohol; PSCs, perovskite solar cells; FESEM, field emission scanning electron microscopy; PCE, power conversion efficiency.

(a) PCE of UV-degraded devices is normalized with respect to PCE of fresh n-i-p triple cation PSCs and is plotted as a function of UV exposure time with and without filter PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%); (b) comparison of normalized values of J-V characteristics of n-i-p triple cation PSCs exposed to UV light for 900 h with and without PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter; (c) PCE of light degraded devices is normalized with respect to PCE of fresh n-i-p triple cation PSCs and is plotted as a function of light exposure time with and without PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter; and (d) comparison of normalized values of J-V characteristics of n-i-p triple cation PSCs exposed to AM1.5 solar spectra light for 1,000 h with and without PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter. PCE, power conversion efficiency; PSCs, perovskite solar cells; PUF, PANI UV filter; CQDs, carbon quantum dots.

Shows the trap density of states(tDOS) vs. energy level(Eω) of triple cation PSCs(n-i-p) before and after degradation in N2 environment in which (a) comparison of trap density of states vs. Eω of fresh and degraded devices in UV light after 900 h with and without PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter; and (b) comparison of trap density of states vs. Eω of fresh and degraded devices in AM1.5 solar radiation after 1,100 h with and without PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter. PUF, PANI UV filter; CQDs, carbon quantum dots; PSCs, perovskite solar cells.

The minimum and maximum values of tDOS in each of the three bands of tDOS vs Eω graphs for fresh and degraded triple cation PSCs exposed to UV and 1-sun radiation for 900 h and 1,000 h respectively. The first band lies below 0.20 eV, the second band lies from 0.20 eV to 0.40 eV and the third band lies above 0.50 eV.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Power conversion efficiency (PCE) of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has reached 26.1%, but PSC devices are plagued by poor stability when exposed to light (especially ultraviolet (UV) radiation), heat, and moisture. UV stability remains a significant challenge to overcome. Luminescent down-shifting (LDS) filters have shown significant enhancement in photostability and efficiency for PSCs. However, most explored LDS materials are costly, non-biodegradable, and the resulting photostability is limited to ∼100 h. In this report, as-obtained waste filtrate from the polyaniline (PANI) synthesis is used to synthesize fluorescent PANI carbon quantum dots (PANI-CQDs) using a facile hydrothermal method. Here we report, for the first time, the use of waste-derived PANI-CQDs to fabricate UV filters that are low-cost, bio-degradable, and room-temperature processible and, importantly, impart high UV and photostability to the PSCs. PSCs with these filters retained 90% and 100% of their initial performance when exposed to UV light and AM 1.5 solar radiation, respectively, for more than 900 h, while PSCs without filters degraded to 14 and 70% of their initial performance under the same conditions. Hence, we clearly show that using a waste-derived LDS filter improves the UV stability of PSCs by six times and photostability beyond 1,000 h.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past ten years, the use of solar photovoltaics (PV) as a clean and renewable energy source has gained substantial momentum, allowing for a quicker transition to clean energy in the efforts to mitigate global warming. While silicon is the primary material used in most commercial solar PV panels [1], perovskite solar cells (PSCs) based on organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite materials have become increasingly popular in recent years [2]. Their photo conversion efficiency (PCE) has increased dramatically from a meager 3.8% in 2009 [3] to 26.1% in 2021 [4]. Perovskites are a class of organic-inorganic compounds having a distinct ABX3 structure and excellent PV properties [5,6]. These properties include a high absorption coefficient [7], a long charge carrier lifetime [8], a high carrier mobility [9], and a low non-radiative recombination rate [9]. They have thus become popular as a light-absorbing layer in solar cells [10]. The high PCE, excellent PV properties, solution processibility, simplicity of manufacturing, and low processing cost of the devices are responsible for the meteoric increase in attention this technology has received from the scientific community over the past decade [11–15].

However, the performance of PSCs is plagued by poor operational stability upon exposure to external elements such as moisture, heat, and light [16,17], which leads to fast degradation in device performance and results in difficulties for commercialization. This degradation due to moisture and oxygen exposure can be alleviated by proper encapsulation [18], capping by a two-dimensional (2D) layer [19], defect reduction by additives [20], and grain-boundary passivation [21]. Till now, different types of carbon materials (like graphene, carbon nanotube, and fullerene) have been used in the interior of the PSCs, along with photo-absorber of perovskite material, charge transport layer, and electrode of PSCs to improve the efficiency and moisture stability [22]. However, the study of device stability in ultraviolet (UV) remains elusive, and one of the most important challenges to overcome [23]. In particular, devices with a titanium dioxide (TiO2) electron transport layer (ETL) have been reported to reduce device stability under UV illumination in several ways. Firstly, the photocatalytic effect at the surface of TiO2 is reported to decompose the methylammonium lead iodide (CH3NH3PbI3) perovskite to lead iodide (PbI2) in an irreversible process. Secondly, upon UV exposure, deep trapping of carriers at oxygen vacancies on the TiO2 surface is reported to strongly enhance carrier recombination and, thus, decrease the performance of PSCs [24]. Ito et al. suggested that the formation of iodide(I2) due to electron trapping by deep trap states at the TiO2/perovskite interface can decompose the perovskite by evaporation of volatile compounds [17]. Similarly, in mixed halide perovskites, light soaking leads to phase segregation due to ion migration, as MAPb(Ix-3Brx)3 converts into iodine(I)-rich and bromine(Br)-rich crystalline phases [25]. Even though triple-cation perovskites exhibit superior moisture and thermal stability, they are also prone to photo-degradation owing to light-induced halide migration and segregation. Governed by Fick's first law, I2 ions formed during UV degradation in PSCs diffuse from perovskite film to other layers due to a large concentration gradient [26], and similarly, perovskite films degrade due to the diffusion of silver (Ag) metal into perovskite film [27]. The stability of PSCs is also compromised by the presence of organic components (organic cation of perovskite, organic hole transport, and electron transport layers) that can decompose upon exposure to high-energy UV light [28]. Over the years, several techniques have been adopted to mitigate UV-induced degradation, such as replacing TiO2 with other benign materials [29], depositing blocking layers between the TiO2/perovskite interface to avoid electron exchange [17], use of a redox shuttle layer [30], as well as the use of either a UV filter or a luminescent down-shifting (LDS) layer that prevents UV radiation from reaching the cell. As in recently published reports, Junjie Jin et al. used YVO4:Eu3+, Bi3+ LDS material, which emits red light after absorption of UV light into the mesoporous TiO2 to improve the efficiency as well as UV stability (365 nm) for 60 h, which retains only 70% PCE in ambient conditions [31]. The use of redox pair shuttles of Eu3+ - Eu2+ and Gd3+ - Gd2+ exhibited significant enhancement in the photostability and thermal stability of PSCs [32–35]. These redox shuttles only protect the perovskite film, while other parts, like the ETL layer of TiO2 and the organic hole transport layer (HTL), remain unprotected from photodegradation. Recently, Lin et al. improved the photostability of PSCs by the construction of an ion diffusion barrier of network structure silicone resin between perovskite and the HTL. The small Si–O–Si unit of silicone resin formed Si–I and Pb–O with the perovskite surface, which physically and chemically inhibited diffusion and self-release of iodine [36]. Whereas Bi et al. employed a diffusion-inhibiting nanocarbon layer (combination of graphene derivative, fullerene derivative, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) between perovskite and Ag electrode [28], that results in photostability enhancement by suppressing I2 ion diffusion or Ag ion diffusion [13]. However, the diffusion barrier approach is limited to mitigation of degradation due to the diffusion of ions.

To overcome the challenge of device degradation due to UV-irradiation, people have also used interfacial modification using nanoparticles such as Ye et al. improved the photostability of PSCs by using hydrothermally synthesized nanoparticles (NPs) of NaTaO3 as an ETL in place of the TiO2 layer that also works as a UV absorber layer. However, it did not yield any fluorescence in the visible region after absorbing UV light and was limited to n-i-p configuration [37].

Among all the reported approaches to counter photodegradation in solar cell devices, LDS has been proven as an essential tool to improve the performance in PSCs [7], organic solar cells [38,39], as well as silicon solar cells [40], as it can effectively filter out ultraviolet A (UVA) and ultraviolet B (UVB) radiations. LDS helps regain the lost current generated by irradiated UV photons by converting UV to Visible light. LDS layer can be easily coated on the back side of solar cells using screen printing or doctor-blade methods and can be easily detached or changed if the need arises [41,42]. Y. G. Tu et al. used a UV filter made of polyimide in space to study the photostability of a mixed PSC device (area 1 cm2) at AM0 illumination at an altitude of 35 km in space [43]. However, the testing results were only limited to 100 min illumination where the cells retained 95.19% of initial PCE in space. S. S. Azad et al. used multifunctional inorganic materials like aluminum-doped zinc oxide (A-ZnO) and ZnO as a UV protective layer that retained 40% and 30% of initial PCE, respectively, after being exposed to UV light (50 mW/cm2), but the UV exposure was only performed for 100 hr in an ambient atmosphere [44]. Similarly, H. S. Anizelli et al. used Kremer blue dispersed in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) matrix as LDS (protecting UV range 350 nm–420 nm), but it was not able to convert UV light into visible and exhibited poor photostability with PCE retention of 30 hr in UV light [45]. By following a similar approach, D. Zhang et al. used CQDs fabricated from citric acid and ethylenediamine mixed in deionized (DI) water by hydrothermal method on the exterior and interfacial parts of PSCs; however, the photostability of modified triple cation PSCs was only monitored for 100 hr under AM1.5 solar irradiation [46]. D. Benetti et al. improved the photostability and efficiency of PSCs (p-i-n configuration) by using carbon dots (Cdots) synthesized by the solvothermal method; the composite film of these Cdots and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) improved the photostability of PSCs for only half an hour [47]. A. A. Maxim et al. reported the fabrication of hydrothermally synthesized phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots by using dextrose and Na2HPO4. Furthermore, he did not report any increase in UV and photostability with the use of CQDs [48]. Thus based on recent reports, the use of LDS materials has shown an observable rise in photostability and efficiency enhancement in PSCs. However, most explored LDS materials are costly, non-biodegradable, and the resulting photostability is limited to at the most ∼100 h. Thus, it is imperative to develop LDS that are based on low-cost materials and lead to photostability of at least 1,000 h as a step toward making PSCs commercially viable.

Therefore, in this study, we have synthesized CQDs from waste using a facile technique that has not been reported before and implemented the CQDs in the LDS UV filter. Moreover, we have demonstrated 100% PCE retention in PSCs on employing the CQDs-based UV filter when exposed to 1 sun radiation for as long as 1,000 h, which is the highest photostability reported so far using an LDS filter.

Polyaniline (PANI) has become one of the most studied and synthesized conducting polymers in the last 50 years owing to a vast range of possible applications in corrosion protection, sensors, supercapacitors, conducting paints, and water purification membranes [49,50]. Most laboratory syntheses have a yield of less than 60% for the polymer. This means that more than 40% of the aniline monomer is washed away in filtrate during filtration of the obtained polymer. Usually, this filtrate is discarded as waste due to the large concentration of unwanted acids and salts present along with aniline monomer, which is known to have high toxicity towards both animal and plant life. Therefore, it is only logical to either recycle the filtrate or make use of this unreacted monomer in smarter ways to reduce its harmful impact on the environment. Hence, in this work, we have used the as-obtained waste filtrate during PANI synthesis to produce fluorescent nitrogen(N), sulfur(S), and phosphorus(P)-rich CQDs (PANI-CQDs) using the facile hydrothermal method. Hydrothermal synthesis is an eco-friendly and cost-effective process that has recently been used to fabricate the CQDs [51,52], but till now, there have been no reports of CQDs synthesized from the waste derived during PANI synthesis. The morphology and structural characterization of PANI-CQDs are presented. PANI-CQDs emit blue fluorescence (430 nm) when excited with UV light and exhibit high absorbance in UV as well as good transmittance in the visible range, possessing properties desirable in the LDS layer for solar cell applications. We embedded PANI-CQD in the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to obtain PANI-CQD-PVA thin films. These films were integrated on the back side of mixed cation mixed halide PSCs as LDS layer to test its UV blocking ability for improving the photostability of these cells. So, here we report a low-cost, biodegradable (since both PVA and CQD are biodegradable) [53,54], and room temperature processible CQDs-based filter to improve the UV and photostability of PSCs. PSCs with UV filters retained 90% and 100% of their initial performance when exposed to UV light and AM 1.5 solar radiation, respectively, for more than 900 hr, while PSCs without UV filters degraded to 14% and 70% of their initial performance under the same conditions. For the first time, this study shows that PSCs' UV stability and photostability can be increased by a factor of six and 1.4, respectively, when employing an LDS filter made from waste-derived PANI-CQDs and PVA.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO)-coated substrates of sheet resistance 7 Ω/cm2 were purchased from Global Nanotech Pvt. Ltd. (GNPL). Aniline, phosphoric acid, ammonium persulfate, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), ethyl acetate, acetic acid, ethanol, lead iodide (PbI2), lead bromide (PbBr2), cesium iodide (CsI), titanium iso-prop oxide (TIPO), dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Spiro-OMeTAD, chlorobenzene, tert-butyl pyridine (TBP), Li-TFSI salt, and acetonitrile were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Zinc dust and sodium hydroxide (KOH) were purchased from Loba Chemie. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and soap solution were purchased from Fisher Scientific. Formamidinium iodide (FAI) was purchased from Greatcell Solar Materials, and methyl ammonium bromide (MABr) was procured from TCI.
b
Synthesis of PANI-CQDs and UV filter:

First, 2.86 ml aniline was added dropwise to 80 ml of DI water and was mixed by stirring on a hot plate at room temperature for 10 min. Then, 2 ml phosphoric acid, diluted with 10 ml DI water, was added dropwise by burette and mixed for 15 min. When a yellowish color appeared, the solution of 3.5 g ammonium persulfate in 10 ml DI water was added. After stirring the above solution overnight at room temperature, it was filtered by using a 125 mm Whatman filter paper. A 50 ml of filtered solution was taken in a Teflon pot of autoclave, and it was placed inside the oven for 16 h at 200 °C. The temperature of the oven was reduced to room temperature, and the pH value of PANI-CQDs was maintained at 7 by dropwise addition of diluted KOH solution. This solution was taken in a solvent extraction flask and shaken well with 35 ml of ethyl acetate. After 5 min, salt and acid remained in the water while CQDs were extracted into ethyl acetate. After removing the water, 2.5 ml of acetic acid was added to the solution of CQDs, followed by the addition of 32 ml of DI water after 2–3 min whilst shaking vigorously. Finally, PANI-CQDs dispersed in DI water were collected and filtered with a 0.22 μm pore-size polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filter. Filtered PANI-CQDs were placed in an oven for drying at 70 °C. The synthesis of PANI-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. S1.

60 mg of PVA was added to 2.48 mg of PANI-CQDs in 1 ml DI water to prepare 3.98 wt % PANI-CQDs solution and was kept overnight on a hot plate for stirring. The solution was then spin-coated on the cleaned glass substrate of size 2.5 cm × 2.5 cm at 100 rpm for 30 s. The glass substrate was then kept for drying at room temperature as shown in Fig. S2(a) to make the PANI UV filter (PUF) with PANI-CQDs concentration of 3.98%, which will be referred to as PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%). Similarly, PANI UV Filters (PUFs) with 5.68, 7.76, and 11.22 wt% of PANI-CQDs were synthesized and are denoted in short form as PUF(CQDs-5.68 wt%), PUF(CQDs-7.76 wt%) and PUF(CQDs-11.22 wt%). PUF made of only PVA without PANI-CQDs will be referred to as PUF(CQDs-0wt%).
c
Fabrication of perovskite solar cells:

The pattering of the FTO-coated glass substrate of dimension (2.5 cm × 2.5 cm) was done by etching using zinc dust and diluted 4 M HCl. Then, the patterned substrates were sequentially cleaned with sonication for 10 min in each solution, i.e. soap solution, DI water, hot DI water, acetone, hot isopropanol (IPA), and IPA, followed by drying with N2 gas. To make the solution of ETL of compact TiO2 (c-TiO2), 7.25 ml ethanol, 500 μL TIPO, and 70 μL concentrated HCl were mixed by stirring on a hot plate at room temperature for 30 min. A filtered solution of c-TiO2 (using a 0.22-μm pore size PTFE filter) was then used for spin coating at 4,000 rpm for 40 s time. Annealing of coated films on a hot plate was done first at 130 °C for 10 min and then at 500 °C for 30 min PbI2 (0.507 g), PbBr2 (0.073 g), FAI (0.172 g), and MABr (0.022 g) were mixed in one ml of DMF and DMSO solution with a 4:1 ratio and stirred overnight at room temperature. 84 micro-liters of 1.5 M CsI solution were added to the above solution and stirred for 3 h to form the triple-cation perovskite solution. 0.22-μm pore size PTFE filter was used to filter the stirred solution of perovskite. The filtered solution was used to make the thin film of triple cation perovskite (Cs0.1(MA0.12FA0.88)0.90Pb(Io.95Br0.05 )) of 300 nm thickness with 1.62 eV band gap on the c-TiO2 substrate by following the two-step spin coating process: first step at 1,000 rpm for 10 s and the second step at 4,000 rpm for 45 s with 300 μL chlorobenzene dropped in the last 15 s as an antisolvent. These films were annealed on a hot plate in two steps: first at 130 °C for 40 min and second step at 100 °C for 20 min inside an N2-filled glove box. Spiro-OMeTAD was used as an HTL in the n-i-p structure. 73.4 mg Spiro-OMeTAD in 1 ml of chlorobenzene was mixed by stirring on a hot plate at 30 °C for 4 h. Then 17.7 microliters Li-TFSI (530 mg Li-TFSI in one ml of acetonitrile) and 28.8 microliters TBP were added to the above solution and stirred for 1 h at 30 °C. The solution was then spin-coated at 3,000 rpm for 30 s on the perovskite layer. To make the anode of 2 mm width on this n-i-p device, 99.9% pure gold (Au) of 100 nm thickness at a rate of 0.1–0.2 Å/s was deposited using a shadow mask to maintain 6 mm2 pixel area by thermal evaporation.
d
Material and device characterization:

High-resolution scanning transmission electron microscopy (HRSTEM) of FEI Titan G2 60-300 KV transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to record the image of CQD NPs. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were taken from FEI NOVA NANO SEM 450. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and Kelvin probe force microscopy (AFM, Asylum MFP-3D) were used to measure the surface morphology of films. A profilometer (Bruker's DektakXT stylus) was used for film thickness measurement. X'Pert Pro PAN analytical diffractometer equipped with a monochromatic CuKα radiation source (λ = 1.5405 Å) was used to obtain X-ray diffraction patterns (2θ scan) of powder materials and films coated on a substrate. Princeton Instruments Acton Spectra Pro 2500i) equipped with a 532 nm diode-pumped solid-state (DPSS) laser was used to obtain the Raman spectra. Optical absorption properties of the films were collected using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 5000 UV-VIS-NIR of series UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer). Photoluminescence of the films was measured using a JASCO FP8550 Spectrofluorometer with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm for perovskite films and 365 nm for PUFs, respectively. Perkin Elmer (Spectrum 2-UATR) was used to obtain the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra. Impedance spectroscopy (Agilent 16048H) was used to measure the capacitance of triple cation PSCs in the frequency range (40Hz–100000Hz) and voltage (−1.2 V to 1.2 V). J-V characteristics of the fabricated devices were measured using a Keithley 2420 source measurement unit under a simulated AM 1.5G spectrum (100 mWcm−2) with a standard xenon lamp-based solar simulator (Oriel 9600 solar simulator), which was calibrated with monocrystalline silicon reference solar cell equipped with KG-5 visible color filter. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) of PSCs was measured using a Newport IQE200 system.

To confirm the formation of PANI-CQDs after hydrothermal synthesis, the PANI-CQDs solution was placed on a UV lamp of 365 nm wavelength to examine the emission of blue light after absorption of UV light, as shown in Fig. S1(c).

SEM images of fluorescent PANI-CQDs obtained after hydrothermal treatment are presented in Fig. 1 (a). HRSTEM image, as shown in Fig. 1(b), reveals spherical morphology with individual particles having a size of around ∼10 nm along with the agglomerates of ∼100 nm, as evident from Fig. 1(a). The polymerization of aniline, along with carbonization, leads to the formation of these CQDs. As the filtrate is set for hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C, polymerization of aniline dominates the initial stages. However, as time increases, the carbonization of aniline starts to dominate the reaction dynamics, ultimately leading to the formation of CQDs through bottom-up synthesis [55]. This argument is substantiated by the similarity in the FTIR spectra of PANI-CQDs (Fig. 2 (a)) with that of pristine PANI polymer (Fig. S4(b)). The peaks at 1568 cm−1 and 1489 cm−1 represent the C=N and C=C stretching modes of quinoid and benzenoid rings, respectively, present in the PANI polymer, as shown in Fig. S4(b). Similarly, the peak at 1640 cm−1 (Fig. 2(a)) represents the C=N stretching mode present in the core of PANI-CQDs, which also confirms the derivative of aniline present in PANI-CQDs [56,57]. PANI powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of pristine PANI (Fig. S3(a)) and Fig. S3(b) of PANI-CQDs also show a common peak at 2θ = 25.5° indicating the formation of hybrid PANI-CQD structure. HRSTEM images (Fig. 1(b-d)) of PANI-CQDs reveal nanocrystalline domains with lattice spacings of ca. 0.24 nm, 0.28 nm, and 0.32 nm, corresponding to 112, 020, and 002 planes, respectively, indicating a graphitic structure [58]. The SAED pattern obtained for PANI-CQDs shows bright spots due to the presence of crystalline graphitic CQDs [58]. The lattice spacing observed in HRSTEM images also corroborated well with peaks obtained in XRD spectra (2θ = 25.5°), corresponding to the 002 plane of graphitic structures. The Raman spectrum (Fig. S3(c)) has typical characteristics of PANI-CQDs with G and D bands at ∼1530 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1, respectively, signifying the presence of ordered crystalline graphitic sp2 carbon core with sp2 carbon defects on the surface or in the cores [58]. The ratio of intensities of D and G bands (ID/IG < 1) observed in Raman spectra of PANI-CQDs is associated with highly crystalline/polycrystalline CQDs [58]. The optical properties of the PANI-CQDs were also measured, including UV-VIS absorbance, which showed the PANI-CQDs film on a glass substrate absorbs UV radiation in the range of 300–400 nm (Fig. S5(b)). It also showed good transmission (>80%) in the visible region, as shown in Fig. S5(c). Moreover, photoluminescence (PL) of the CQDs film indicated a PL peak at 460 nm (Fig. S5(d)), which is red-shifted owing to the agglomeration of CQDs deposited from water on the glass slide. This is mitigated by incorporating CQDs in PVA, and the results are presented in the subsequent section.

To demonstrate the application of PANI-CQDs as an LDS layer, we fabricated PANI-CQDs-PVA composite thin films, referred to as PUFs henceforth. PUF films retain the optical transparency and photoluminescence (PL) properties of PANI-CQDs, as also discussed in the subsequent section. Thicknesses of PUF films were in the range of 100–105 μm. PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) had a thickness of 102 μm which was also confirmed by the FESEM image as shown in Fig. 5(b). AFM images shown in Fig. 1(e and f) depict the surface topography of PUF films at the two different PANI-CQDs concentrations of 13.95 wt% and 3.98 wt%, respectively, clearly illustrating the uniform dispersion of PANI-CQDs in PUF films where the packing density of the PANI-CQDs increased on increasing the concentration. Fig. 2(b) shows the FTIR spectra of a thin film of PUF having a composite of PANI-CQDs and PVA, where the C=N stretching at 1708 cm−1 wavenumber confirms the presence of PANI-CQDs and C–H stretching at 2920 cm−1, which corresponds to PVA as also seen in Fig. S4(a). Fig. 2(c) shows the absorbance of PUF films with different concentrations of PANI-CQDs. Strong absorbance in the UVA and the UVB region of the spectra effectively covers the entire spectral range of AM1.5G solar spectra in the UV region. The peak at 295 nm is usually assigned to π- π∗ transitions in C=N bonds in PANI-CQDs cores, while the absorption from 350 to 400 nm is due to π- π∗ transitions of various surface functional groups present [59,60]. A weak broad absorption band extending in the visible region is also observed, which may be due to the extended π - π conjugation [61]. When the concentration of PANI-CQDs is increased from 3.98 wt% to 11.22 wt%, the absorption of UV light at 291–400 nm is significantly increased, as shown in Fig. 2(c), whereas the filter without PANI-CQD (0 wt%) showed very little absorption in the UV region. These films show excellent transmittance in the visible region of spectra, as shown in Fig. S4(d), where PSCs have high EQE, as shown in Fig. 5(e). Particularly, PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) film exhibited >80% transmission in the wavelength range of >400 nm. PUF films exhibit bright blue (∼430 nm) fluorescence; fluorescence excitation vs. emission spectra for different excitation wavelengths in the UV region from 295 nm–390 nm are presented in Fig. 2(d). PUF films show maximum emission intensity with an excitation wavelength between 360 nm and 380 nm, which is encouraging as these wavelengths account for a large proportion of UV content in the AM 1.5G spectrum. The PL spectra of PUF films are also measured as function of PANI-CQDs concentrations and are shown in Fig. S4(c). PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) has more PL compared to higher concentrations of CQDs (5.68% and 7.76%), probably due to the agglomeration of PANI-CQDs beyond 3.98 wt% leading to quenching of PL [62,63].

In order to minimize the parasitic absorption by the filter in the visible range so that more visible light photons reach the PSC, we used PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter (which exhibited >80% transmission in the visible region and highest PL intensity) for subsequent testing with the solar cells. Characterization of the PUF films leads us to important inferences. Carbon dots are known to be excellent photo-stable nanomaterial over prolonged exposure periods in UV light [64,65]. Rudko et al. showed that the incorporation of inorganic CdS NPs in PVA strongly diminished the harmful effects of UV light [66]. The suggested sources of the UV-protective action of the NPs include the following: (i) shielding of the polymeric matrix from the incident radiation by NPs via absorption of UV light, i.e. the lowering of the efficiency of UV excitation of the polymer; and (ii) de-excitation of the already excited macromolecules via energy drain to NPs. Similar phenomena regarding stability could be expected from PUF films over prolonged UV and light exposure periods. The stability results of the PUF filters are summarized in Fig. S6, suggesting superior UV stability of filters for >1,000 h when continuously illuminated with a UV lamp of 6 W under 1 sun illumination in N2 environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and photostability of perovskite films
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the direct effect of UV light of the solar spectrum on perovskite film, the triple-cation perovskite films of thickness 300 nm were deposited on c-TiO2 films and were exposed to UV light and AM1.5 spectrum of white LED light without and with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter in N2 environment (O2 < 0.5 ppm (avg), H2O = 0.1 ppm). The absorbance of these films, in the range of 350 nm–800 nm, was recorded at regular intervals of time during exposure to UV and white LED light, as shown in Fig. S7. The fresh film had a bandgap of 1.62 eV, which reduced to 1.55 eV without PUF on UV exposure for 800 h, while the film with PUF exhibited a higher bandgap of 1.57 eV when degraded under the same condition (Fig. S8(a)). Similarly, under white light degradation (1 sun exposure), the bandgap degraded to 1.58 eV without PUF, and the perovskite film with PUF retained a higher value of E
g = 1.60 eV after exposure (Fig. S8(b)). Under the effect of UV illumination, photoinduced halide migration leads to the formation of halide vacancies as shallow defect states close to the band edge, reducing the effective band gap [67]. Hence, a higher bandgap after degradation is an indication of lower decomposition of perovskite in the films with PUF. Fig. S7 also indicates slower degradation of the perovskite films protected by PUF when compared to films without PUF under both UV and 1 sun exposure and is signified by the rate of change of absorption with exposure time. To summarize, PUF mitigated the degradation in perovskite films from UV and white light, as suggested by the photophysical measurements.

To study the effect of photodegradation on the morphology of perovskite films, AFM measurements were performed on fresh and degraded films, which are shown in Fig. S9. An increase in the roughness from 31.7 nm in the pristine state to the final roughness of 47.6 nm and 34.8 nm was observed in perovskite films without and with PUF, respectively, on exposure to UV light for 800 h. Similarly, after 1 sun degradation for 1,100 h, the roughness of the perovskite film was limited to 35.3 nm, while it increased to 48.2 nm without PUF protection. A far larger increase in the roughness of perovskite film without PUF indicates the formation of pin holes in the perovskite film owing to the halide movement and segregation in response to light [68]. This segregation causes the formation of defects in perovskite, accelerating the rate of degradation. This finding is substantiated by the SEM micrographs discussed in the later section.

Fermi levels of the perovskite films were determined by Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM), which measures the spatial contact potential difference (VCPD) between the tip and sample. The fresh triple-cation perovskite film had a root mean square (RMS) surface potential of 198.3 mV, as shown in Fig. S10(a), but after degradation in UV light, its value reached 21.5 mV without PUF, as shown in Fig. S10(b) due to the pinning of Fermi energy level to shallow and intermediate trap energy level [69]. A relatively higher value of the surface potential of 77.6 mV was obtained for perovskite film (Fig. S10(c)) due to a smaller shift of the Fermi level, indicating a lower number of defect states in the bandgap. A similar effect was observed for degradation under one sun condition, as illustrated in Figs. S10(d and e), which confirms that PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) offers a stability boost to perovskite films and mitigates the formation of sub-bandgap defects under UV and 1 sun exposure.

The FESEM image of a fresh triple cation perovskite film, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), has an average grain size of ∼300 nm, as shown in the histogram of Fig. S11(a). The white region in the fresh film is due to the undissolved or excess PbI2. When this film was degraded in UV light of 365 nm wavelength without filter for 800 h, its grain size increased to ∼400 nm (Fig. S11(b)) due to photo annealing of perovskite film [70,71]. Many pin holes (highlighted in the red circle of Fig. 3(b)) appeared, and some grains were partially converted to PbI2 (white region inside some grains) after degradation. UV exposure of perovskite film with PUF also concluded with the increase in grain size to ∼400 nm (Fig. S11(c)). However, no pin holes were observed, and the area coverage of the PbI2 white phase also remained low. Likewise, under 1 sun degradation, the film without filter degraded via the formation of pin holes and PbI2 phase, whereas the PUF-protected perovskite film retained its structural integrity. These results corroborate the film stability results of the AFM, KPFM, and photophysical measurements discussed earlier and highlight the photostability imparted by the PUF to the triple-cation perovskite films.

The XRD pattern of the perovskite films is shown in Fig. 4
, showing the peaks at 2θ = 14.09°, 20.05°, 24.55°, 28.41°, 31.89°, 34.95°, 40.65°, and 43.23°, corresponding to (001), (011), (111), (002), (012), (112), (022), and (003) planes, respectively, of triple-cation perovskite [72]. The XRD plots of fresh and degraded perovskite films after 260 h in UV light with and without PUF are shown in Fig. 4(a). The peaks at 2θ = 12.69° and 11.39° in degraded films appear due to PbI2 and δ-phase of triple-cation perovskite, respectively [72]. Fig. 4(b) shows the zoomed-in XRD spectra (between 10° to 15°) of fresh and degraded films in UV light to compare the peaks of α and δ perovskite phase and PbI2. The film with PUF showed a reduction in the PbI2 phase (corroborates SEM results), which was accompanied by an increase in the photoinactive δ-phase on UV degradation whilst preserving the crystallinity of the photoactive α phase (Fig. 4(c)). On the other hand, the film without PUF lost its crystallinity completely owing to the formation of defects and decomposition of perovskite (shown in Fig. 3(b)), as suggested by the broadening of α phase peak in Fig. 4(b). This further proves that the PUF successfully protected the perovskite film against UV degradation and helped retain the photoactive α phase. A similar effect was observed in the degradation of perovskite film upon in one sun solar radiation with PUF (Fig. S12(a)). However, without PUF, a relatively lower decomposition of perovskite film was observed as compared to the UV light for the same exposure time (Fig. S12(b)) which is attributed to the lower intensity of UV light in one sun solar spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and photostability of perovskite solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: PUF of different PANI-CQDs concentrations was placed on the glass surface side of triple cation PSCs of n-i-p configuration (as shown in Fig. 5(a and b), and AM1.5G solar spectrum light was incident during J-V measurement. Fig. 5(c) shows the normalized PCE with respect to no filter condition for the different PUFs when combined with the PCE. The normalized PCEs are ca. 99.67%, 93.52%, 93.21%, 89.31%, and 88.96% for PUF(CQDs-0%), PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%), PUF(CQDs-5.68 wt%), PUF(CQDs-7.76 wt%) and PUF(CQDs-11.22 wt%), respectively. It is observed that as the PANI-CQDs concentration in the PUFs increased, the normalized PCE decreased owing to the increased absorption of visible photons at higher PANI-CQDs concentrations. This was also confirmed by the decreasing absorption in perovskite films on increasing the concentration of CQDs in filters, as shown in Fig. S5(a). A minimum PCE loss of 6.48% is observed for PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%), and hence we have chosen PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) as the suitable filter to study the UV and photostability of PSCs without affecting the PCE of solar cells significantly. The photocurrent density vs. voltage (J-V) curves of the triple cation PSCs, with and without PUF, are plotted in Fig. 5(d) for the reverse scan, where control triple cation PSCs exhibited ∼20.4% PCE efficiency with open circuit voltage (Voc) is 1.03 V and fill factor (FF) is 77.5%, and short circuit current density (Jsc), ∼25.56 mA/cm2. In comparison, the PSCs with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) demonstrated efficiency ∼18.97%, Voc = 1.03 V, FF = 78.1% and Jsc = 23.7 mA/cm2. There is a 7.3% reduction in the short circuit current density on using the PUF and the overall reduction in PCE is ∼6.5%. The EQE spectra (Fig. 5(e)) illustrate the lower spectral response of the PSC with PUF filter compared to the control PSCs in the 300–450 nm band, owing to the absorption by PANI-CQDs (Fig. S4(d) and Fig. S5(c)). The current densities observed from the J-V measurements are in agreement with the integrated current densities obtained from the EQE measurements: 25.2 mA/cm2 for the control device and 23.35 mA/cm2 for the PSCs with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%). These photovoltaic parameters for PSCs are also summarized in Table S1.

The triple cation PSCs devices of n-i-p configuration were exposed to 365 nm UV light and one sun spectrum with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) and their J-V performances and J-V graphs were measured and plotted at regular intervals as shown in Fig. 6 (a, b, c, and d) and Fig. S(14 and 15) to evaluate the degradation of PSCs in an N2 environment at 25 °C. The n-i-p device with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) and control n-i-p device without UV filter were exposed to UV light, and their PCEs after degradation were normalized with respect to the initial PCE and are plotted vs. UV exposure time in Fig. 6(a). On degrading the control device in UV without PUF, the control device almost completely degraded in 900 h as is evident from its final PCE of 14% with respect to initial PCE, implying that it lost 86% of its performance owing to the reduction of FF, Jsc, and Voc by 44.9%, 43.1%, and 85.4%, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(b). However, when the same device was exposed to UV light with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%), the PSC retained 90% of its initial performance after 900 h of UV exposure, implying that it lost only 10% of its initial PCE, owing to very small losses of 8%, 6.30%, and −7.9% in FF, Jsc, and Voc , respectively. This confirms that the PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter significantly boosted the UV stability of triple-cation PSCs to upwards of 900 h.

To study the photostability of n-i-p triple cation PSCs, AM1.5 solar simulated radiation of 1 sun intensity from an LED lamp was illuminated on PSCs in inert conditions of a nitrogen-filled glove box, and J-V characteristics of the PSCs were measured periodically to evaluate the degradation of PSCs under exposure. The n-i-p device with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter and control n-i-p device without UV filter are degraded in the presence of AM1.5 radiation, and their PCE after degradation were normalized with respect to the initial PCE and are plotted vs. exposure time in Fig. 6(c). On degrading the control device in AM 1.5 spectra without filter for 1,000 h, the control degraded to 70% of its initial PCE, implying that it lost 30% of its performance owing to the degradation of FF, Jsc, and Voc by 16%, 14.4%, and 3.20%, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(d). However, when the same device was exposed to AM 1.5 spectra with a PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter, then the device retained 100% of its initial performance after 1,000 h of exposure, implying that the PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter has completely prevented the degradation of triple-cation PSCs for up to 1,000 h of 1 sunlight exposure. Another set of devices was degraded for only 100 h in UV light and 1 sun solar spectra with and without PUF, and a similar observation was made and is plotted in Fig. S16.

To compare the ideality factor of control and with filter after degradation in UV and one sun radiation, the J-V measurements are taken in the dark from −1.2 V to 1.2 V at 0.05 V/s scan rate in the reverse direction. The ideality factor (n) is calculated by measuring the slope of the linear region in ln(J) vs. V graph and using the expression below [73].

where J is current density, V is applied voltage, k is Boltzmann's constant, T is the temperature in Kelvin, and q is the charge of an electron. A high ideality factor of PSC suggests dominant non-radiative recombination due to trap states [74]. The n of the control device increased from 2.75 to 5.62 on degradation in UV light as shown in Fig. S13(a), while n of PSCs with PUF only increased to 4.02 (Fig. S13(b)), indicating a lower defect density in PSCs with PUF. Under 1 sun illumination, the n of the control device reached 3.38 (Fig. S13(c)), and with the filter (Fig. S13(d)) n of PSCs showed a very small increase to 2.51 implying that the PUF filter played a critical role in preventing the decomposition of perovskite and perovskite/charge transport layer interfaces. To further elucidate this effect, the density of defects of PSCs before and after degradation was measured, and the results are discussed in the subsequent section.

The trap density of states (tDOS) of defects in fresh and degraded PSCs in UV and one sun radiation are plotted in Fig. 7 (a and b) and their values are summarized in Table 1 . The trap density of states (tDOS) has been calculated by employing thermal admittance spectroscopy [75–78] using the following equation [79,80]:

Fig. 7 shows the tDOS in fresh devices and degraded devices exposed to UV light for 900 h (Fig. 7(a)) and 1 sun radiation for 1,000 h (Fig. 7(b)). The density of defects is divided into three bands: shallow (0.2–0.4 eV), intermediate (0.4–0.5 eV), and deep (>0.5 eV), and the range of defect density corresponding to these bands for each of the devices is tabulated in Table 1. It is evident from Fig. 7(a) that the PSC degraded with a filter in UV light did not exhibit a significant increase in the defect density, especially in the intermediated and deep regions, while the PSC exposed to UV light without filter protection showed > 6X increase of the defect density in the intermediate band. This further corroborates that the PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter absorbs the harmful UV radiation and prevents the formation of intermediate defects in perovskite such as vacancies, antistites, and interstitials. The use of PUF also protected the c-TiO2/perovskite interface by minimizing the formation of interfacial deep traps. Similar findings are observed for the case of 1 sun radiation exposure on PSCs, i.e. PSC with UV filter showed negligible change in defect density, whereas PSC without filter exhibited >4x increase in defect density, especially in the intermediate and deep bands, which correspond to interfacial traps and high formation energy lattice defects.

This manuscript, for the first time, reports the fabrication of low-cost, biodegradable, room-temperature processable LDS filters that give PSCs excellent UV and photostability using waste-derived PANI-CQDs. The work demonstrates that the utilization of an easy hydrothermal approach to synthesize fluorescent nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) rich PANI carbon quantum dots (PANI-CQDs) from the waste filtrate as-obtained from the polyaniline (PANI) synthesis. Detailed characterization using XRD, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopies suggested that the hydrothermal treatment of waste filtrate led to aniline polymerization during the early stages, followed by aniline carbonization, eventually forming CQDs through bottom-up synthesis. The composite PUF filters of PANI-CQDs and PVA exhibited a substantial increase in the UV light absorption at 291–400 nm when the concentration of PANI-CQDs was increased from 3.98 wt% to 11.22 wt%. Particularly, PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) film showed >80% transmission in the visible region, making it a suitable candidate as an LDS and UV-protecting filter for PSCs.

The triple-cation perovskite films were degraded in the presence of UV light and 1 sun illumination, and the photophysical studies suggested reduced breakdown of perovskite films protected by the UV filter of type PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) as compared to films without filter, also corroborated by a lower rate of absorption decay and reduced reduction in the band gap of the films with PUF filter. AFM, KPFM, XRD, and SEM measurements further substantiated the photostability that the PUF imparts to the triple-cation perovskite films, as the PUF-protected perovskite film maintained its structural integrity under 1 sun and UV light illumination, whereas the film without a filter deteriorated due to the formation of pin holes and PbI2 phase.

Owing to the absorption of the 300–450 nm wavelength range by PANI-CQDs, using the PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) reduced the short circuit current density by 7.3%, while the PCE reduced by ca. 6.5%, also corroborated by the corresponding EQE spectral response of the PSCs with this PUF filter. When exposed to UV light and AM 1.5 solar radiation, respectively, for over 900 h, PSCs with PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) maintained 90% and 100% of their initial performance. In contrast, the performance of PSCs without filters decreased to 14% and 70% of their initial performance in similar conditions. The defect density measurements provided additional evidence that the PUF(CQDs-3.98 wt%) filter inhibited the formation of intermediate band gap defects in perovskite, including vacancies, antistites, and interstitials, by absorbing harmful UV radiation. Utilizing the PUF reduced the creation of interfacial deep traps, safeguarding the c-TiO2/perovskite interface as well.

This study demonstrates for the first time that using an LDS filter created from waste increases PSCs' UV stability by a factor of six and their photostability by a factor of 1.4. One of the main obstacles to the long-term use and commercialization of perovskite devices is the instability caused by UV light exposure. Our study shows a robust low-cost pathway for fabricating stable perovskite solar cells by using waste-derived UV filters. Our work has the potential to pave the way for accelerated commercialization of PSC technology by addressing the PSC stability challenge, which has remained unresolved thus far.

Yugesh Kumar worked on the idea, planned the experiments, performed data collection, conducted detailed characterization, and wrote the original draft. Lokesh Yadav helped in the synthesis and characterization of PANI-CQDs. Ashish Garg and Kanwar Singh Nalwa conceived the project, supervised the project, provided the resources, and contributed to the writing, reviewing, and editing of the manuscript. Raju Kumar Gupta and Anand Singh contributed in the validation and provision of resources. All authors contributed to the finalization of the contents of the manuscript and its final shaping.
